title: Topological ordering

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Topological ordering; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Topological ordering
sources.claim: A topological order linearizes a directed acyclic graph so every edge goes from an earlier to a later vertex.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A topological order linearizes a directed acyclic graph so every edge goes from an earlier to a later vertex.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Kahn's algorithm repeatedly removes zero-indegree vertices and detects a cycle if fewer than V are removed. Your goal is to implement and test Kahn or DFS topological sorting on valid and cyclic inputs. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Graph representation and traversal; Priority queue applications; Breadth-first and depth-first search; Components and cycle detection. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A topological order linearizes a directed acyclic graph so every edge goes from an earlier to a later vertex. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Orders may be nonunique, disconnected DAGs are allowed, and a cycle makes completion impossible. The draft therefore rejects: Every directed graph has at least one topological ordering.

includes: Order a directed acyclic graph and detect when dependencies contain a cycle.
Implement and test Kahn or DFS topological sorting on valid and cyclic inputs.

In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
excludes: Orders may be nonunique, disconnected DAGs are allowed, and a cycle makes completion impossible.

outcomes: Implement and test Kahn or DFS topological sorting on valid and cyclic inputs.

examples.title: Topological ordering: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: Kahn's algorithm repeatedly removes zero-indegree vertices and detects a cycle if fewer than V are removed.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: Kahn's algorithm repeatedly removes zero-indegree vertices and detects a cycle if fewer than V are removed.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Only DAGs have a topological order; incomplete processing or a back edge exposes a cycle.

examples.title: Topological ordering: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Orders may be nonunique, disconnected DAGs are allowed, and a cycle makes completion impossible.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.
